column1: # ZAP Scanning Rules Configuration
# Format: RULE_ID<TAB>THRESHOLD<TAB>[IGNORE/comment]
# Thresholds: IGNORE, INFO, WARN, FAIL, PASS
# When a rule appears multiple times ZAP uses the LAST entry — every rule
# below is listed exactly once.
# Rule names verified against https://www.zaproxy.org/docs/alerts/.
# Policy:
# - FAIL    — must be fixed; blocks merge.
# - WARN    — surfaces in reports but does not block; track separately.
# - INFO    — reported only as informational signal.
# - IGNORE  — scanner artifact or false positive; MUST have justification.
# Every IGNORE entry below explains why it is not a real defect.
# FAIL — critical findings; must not exist in merged code
# --- Injection ---
# --- Transport / mixed content ---
# --- Session / cookie security ---
# --- CSP / security headers (remediated in Phases 1-3) ---

column4: # CSP — wildcard directive, unsafe-inline, etc. (fixed Phase 1)
# Permissions Policy Header Not Set (wired Phase 1)
# Sub Resource Integrity Attribute Missing (fixed Phase 2)
# Insufficient Site Isolation — COEP / COOP (fixed Phase 1)

column1: # --- SSRF / cloud metadata ---
# --- CSRF (we ship a custom CSRF layer; passive rule often misses it) ---
# --- Authentication bypass (active scan) ---
# WARN — surfaced in reports; tracked, not blocking

column4: # Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
# Storable but Non-Cacheable Content (handled Phase 3; keep as signal)
# Unexpected Content-Type — SPA root legitimately returns HTML

column1: # INFO — surfaced only, no expected action
# IGNORE — scanner artifacts or intentional design choices.
# Each entry requires a justification below.
# Private IP disclosure fires on any 10.x / 192.168.x string, including
# documented examples in the Swagger / api-info payloads. Not a defect.
# Modern browsers rely on CSP; the legacy X-XSS-Protection header is set by
# addSecurityHeaders anyway. Rule is historically noisy and does not reflect
# current best practice.

column4: # Web Browser XSS Protection Not Enabled (legacy; CSP covers it)

column1: # Passive open-redirect detection is very false-positive heavy on SPA routes.
# The active-scan counterpart (40028 URL Redirector Abuse) still enforces.

column4: # Open Redirect (passive; active rule 40028 still enforced)

column1: # Rule 10094 fires on any base64 string ≥24 chars. Phase 2 introduces SRI
# hashes (sha384-...) in dist/index.html which are base64 SHA-384 digests.
# SRI hashes are cryptographic PUBLIC material by design — they are meant
# to be in the HTML. Silencing this rule does not silence real secret
# disclosure: PII (10062), hash-value disclosure (10097), and suspicious
# comments (10027) still fire if a real secret leaks.

column4: # Base64 Disclosure (SRI hashes / CSRF tokens are base64 by design)

column1: # ZAP detects that the app is a modern SPA. That is detection, not a
# finding. Nothing to remediate.

column4: # Modern Web Application (SPA detection, not a defect)

column1: # Sec-Fetch-Dest / Mode / Site / User are REQUEST headers that real browsers
# send; ZAP does not send them. Every passive scan therefore flags them as
# "missing" on the outbound response. There is nothing a server can do to
# make a scanner emit these request headers.

column4: # Sec-Fetch-* request headers missing (browser-only, ZAP never sends them)

column1: # ZAP probes cloud-metadata and framework-specific paths like
# /actuator/health, /computeMetadata/v1/, /latest/meta-data/, /metadata/instance.
# We serve 404 for unknown paths, which is the CORRECT response. Rule 100000
# ("Client Error response code") flags that correctness as a finding.

column4: # Client Error 4xx on unknown paths (correct behavior)
